question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where offer.market.loanToken is a fee-on-transfer token charging fee_rate on every transferFrom, trigger take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiver, address(0), '') causing two safeTransferFrom calls (lines 455-456 of Midnight.sol) each to deduct fee_rate from the transferred amount, while claimableSettlementFee is incremented by the full buyerAssets - sellerAssets (line 418) before any transfer, violating the invariant that contract token balances must cover claimableSettlementFee + withdrawable + collateral, causing scoped impact: protocol insolvency where claimableSettlementFee exceeds actual loan token balance? Proof idea: deploy FeeOnTransferLoanToken with 1% fee; create market; fill offer; assert midnight.claimableSettlementFee(loanToken) > loanToken.balanceOf(address(midnight)).

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: repay / SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged borrower, under preconditions where offer.market.loanToken is a fee-on-transfer token, trigger repay(market, units, onBehalf, address(0), '') which credits marketState[id].withdrawable += units (line 509) but only receives units * (1 - fee_rate) via safeTransferFrom (line 520), violating the invariant that withdrawable must be backed by actual token balance, causing scoped impact: lenders can withdraw more than the protocol holds, draining funds from other depositors? Proof idea: fuzz fee_rate in [1, 1000] bps; repay N units; assert withdrawable increase > actual balance increase.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: supplyCollateral / SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged borrower, under preconditions where a collateral token is fee-on-transfer, trigger supplyCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf) which sets position[id][onBehalf].collateral[collateralIndex] = oldCollateral + assets (line 533) but only receives assets * (1 - fee_rate) via safeTransferFrom (line 545), violating the invariant that collateral accounting must match actual token balance held, causing scoped impact: borrower borrows against phantom collateral, creating bad debt? Proof idea: deploy FeeOnTransferCollateral; supply 1000 units; assert position.collateral > midnight.balanceOf(collateralToken); then borrow at LLTV limit.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged maker, under preconditions where loanToken is fee-on-transfer and offer.buy is true with a buyerCallback, trigger take() where the two separate safeTransferFrom calls on lines 455-456 each incur a fee independently (total fee = fee_rate * buyerAssets - sellerAssets + fee_rate * sellerAssets = fee_rate * buyerAssets), while the protocol only accounts for a single net transfer, violating the invariant that token balance delta equals accounting delta, causing scoped impact: double-fee deduction from payer with only single-fee accounting credit? Proof idea: fuzz buyerAssets and sellerAssets with fee_rate > 0; assert sum of two transfer fees != single-transfer fee on full buyerAssets.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged market creator, under preconditions where offer.ratifier points to a contract that always reverts in isRatified, trigger a permanent DoS on all take() calls for that offer, since take() requires isRatified == CALLBACK_SUCCESS (line 356) and a revert propagates as RatifierFail, violating the invariant that valid offers must remain fillable, causing scoped impact: fund freeze where maker's capital is locked in an offer that can never be filled? Proof idea: deploy RevertingRatifier; create offer with ratifier = RevertingRatifier; assert take() always reverts with RatifierFail.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker, under preconditions where a ratifier is deployed that returns bytes32(0) instead of CALLBACK_SUCCESS, trigger take() to revert with RatifierFail even when the maker has authorized the ratifier via isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier], violating the invariant that authorized ratifiers must be able to approve valid offers, causing scoped impact: fund freeze where all offers using this ratifier are permanently blocked? Proof idea: deploy ZeroReturnRatifier; authorize it; call take(); assert revert is RatifierFail not RatifierUnauthorized.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged attacker, under preconditions where isRatified is declared external view (called via STATICCALL) but the ratifier implementation uses assembly to attempt a state write (SSTORE), trigger the STATICCALL to revert due to EVM STATICCALL restrictions, causing take() to revert with RatifierFail for all offers using that ratifier, violating the invariant that ratifier authorization must not be bypassable or freezable, causing scoped impact: low-cost DoS on all takes for affected offers? Proof idea: deploy AssemblySstoreRatifier; authorize it; call take(); assert revert propagates from STATICCALL violation.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier reads Midnight's consumed[offer.maker][offer.group] state inside isRatified (which is called BEFORE consumed is updated at lines 366-373), trigger a ratifier that incorrectly validates an offer as having remaining capacity when it is actually at the limit, violating the invariant that consumed state must be checked before ratification, causing scoped impact: offer overfill where consumed exceeds maxUnits/maxAssets? Proof idea: deploy ConsumedCheckRatifier that reads consumed; fill offer to limit; attempt one more fill; assert ratifier sees stale consumed value.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: flashLoan / SafeTransferLib] Can an unprivileged attacker, under preconditions where a token in the flashLoan tokens array is fee-on-transfer, trigger flashLoan(tokens, assets, callback, data) where safeTransfer sends assets[i] to callback (line 743) but safeTransferFrom pulls back only assets[i] * (1 - fee_rate) (line 750), violating the invariant that flashLoan must be balance-neutral for the protocol, causing scoped impact: protocol insolvency where each flash loan permanently drains fee_rate * assets from the protocol? Proof idea: deploy FeeOnTransferToken; call flashLoan with it; assert midnight.balanceOf(token) decreases by fee_rate * assets per call.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: withdraw / SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer] Can an unprivileged lender, under preconditions where loanToken.transfer() returns false (non-reverting false return), trigger withdraw(market, units, onBehalf, receiver) where SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer reverts with TransferReturnedFalse (line 21 of SafeTransferLib.sol), permanently freezing the lender's credit since position.credit was already decremented (line 493) before the transfer, violating the invariant that valid withdrawals must not be permanently blocked, causing scoped impact: fund freeze where lender credit is burned but tokens are not received? Proof idea: deploy FalseReturnToken; call withdraw; assert revert is TransferReturnedFalse; assert credit was decremented before revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: withdrawCollateral / SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer] Can an unprivileged borrower, under preconditions where collateralToken.transfer() reverts (e.g., blacklisted receiver), trigger withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf, receiver) where position.collateral is decremented and health check passes (lines 561-568) but safeTransfer reverts (line 572), causing the entire transaction to revert and leaving the borrower unable to withdraw collateral to a blacklisted address, violating the invariant that exit paths must remain available, causing scoped impact: fund freeze for borrowers whose receiver is blacklisted? Proof idea: deploy BlacklistCollateralToken; blacklist receiver; call withdrawCollateral; assert revert; assert collateral unchanged due to revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IBuyCallback.onBuy] Can an unprivileged maker, under preconditions where offer.callback (buyerCallback) reverts in onBuy, trigger take() to revert with WrongBuyCallbackReturnValue (line 451), but since consumed[offer.maker][offer.group] was already incremented (lines 366-373) before the callback, the revert rolls back consumed, violating no invariant here, but can a ratifier that reads consumed inside isRatified (called before consumed update) be confused by this ordering, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where ratifier sees pre-update consumed? Proof idea: deploy ConsumedReadingRatifier; fill partially; assert ratifier sees correct consumed at call time.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is a contract that reads offer.market.loanToken.balanceOf(address(midnight)) and reverts if balance is below a threshold, trigger a DoS on all takes when the protocol balance is low (e.g., after large withdrawals), violating the invariant that valid offers must remain fillable regardless of protocol balance, causing scoped impact: low-cost DoS on take() for all offers using this ratifier? Proof idea: deploy BalanceCheckRatifier; drain protocol balance via withdrawals; assert take() reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker, under preconditions where a ratifier is a proxy contract that delegatecalls to an implementation that is later upgraded to always revert, trigger a permanent DoS on all offers using that ratifier after the upgrade, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must remain stable for existing offers, causing scoped impact: fund freeze where all offers using the upgradeable ratifier become permanently unfillable? Proof idea: deploy UpgradeableRatifier; fill offers; upgrade to RevertingImpl; assert all subsequent takes revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is a contract that calls back into Midnight's isAuthorized() view function during isRatified (which is called via STATICCALL), trigger a reentrancy-like read of Midnight state that was set in the same transaction before take() was called, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct maker authorization state, causing scoped impact: gate bypass where ratifier reads stale or manipulated authorization state? Proof idea: deploy AuthReadingRatifier; manipulate isAuthorized before take; assert ratifier reads correct state.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where loanToken has no code (address with no bytecode), trigger take() where SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom reverts with NoCode (line 25 of SafeTransferLib.sol) after all position state has been updated (lines 408-418), causing the entire transaction to revert and rolling back state, violating no invariant (revert is correct), but can a market be created with a no-code loan token address that passes touchMarket, causing scoped impact: permanent fund freeze where the market is created but all takes revert? Proof idea: create market with loanToken = address(0x1234) with no code; call take; assert NoCode revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is a contract that uses gasleft() to conditionally revert (reverts if gas < threshold), trigger selective DoS where low-gas takes revert while high-gas takes succeed, violating the invariant that offer fillability must not depend on gas amount, causing scoped impact: low-cost DoS where attacker can front-run with low-gas transactions to block specific takers? Proof idea: deploy GasCheckRatifier; call take with gas = threshold - 1; assert revert; call with gas = threshold; assert success.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged maker, under preconditions where offer.ratifier == address(0) and isAuthorized[offer.maker][address(0)] is false, trigger take() to revert with RatifierUnauthorized (line 355) since address(0) is not authorized, but if isAuthorized[offer.maker][address(0)] is somehow set to true, trigger IRatifier(address(0)).isRatified() which calls address(0) with no code, causing SafeTransferLib.NoCode-equivalent revert, violating the invariant that zero-address ratifier must be handled safely, causing scoped impact: unexpected revert path for zero-address ratifier? Proof idea: setIsAuthorized(address(0), true, maker); create offer with ratifier=address(0); call take; assert revert behavior.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier returns a bytes32 value that is not CALLBACK_SUCCESS but is also not bytes32(0) (e.g., keccak256('custom')), trigger take() to revert with RatifierFail (line 356), and can this be exploited by a malicious ratifier to selectively block specific takers while allowing others by returning different values based on msg.sender or ratifierData, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must be deterministic for a given offer, causing scoped impact: gate bypass or selective DoS? Proof idea: deploy SelectiveRatifier that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS for maker but bytes32(1) for others; assert taker is blocked.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is a contract that reads block.timestamp and reverts after offer.market.maturity, trigger a DoS on post-maturity takes (which are allowed for unwinding), violating the invariant that post-maturity trading must remain available to facilitate unwinding when liquidations are unprofitable, causing scoped impact: fund freeze where post-maturity debt cannot be unwound? Proof idea: deploy MaturityCheckRatifier; warp past maturity; assert take() reverts; assert debt cannot be reduced.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is a contract that reads EcrecoverRatifier.isRootCanceled state and the maker cancels the root between offer publication and take execution, trigger take() to revert with RatifierFail after the root is canceled, violating the invariant that offer cancellation must be atomic and not leave partial state, causing scoped impact: fund freeze where in-flight takes are blocked after cancellation? Proof idea: sign offer; cancel root; attempt take; assert RatifierFail; assert no state was changed.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is EcrecoverRatifier and the maker's authorization of the ratifier is revoked (setIsAuthorized(ratifier, false, maker)) between offer signing and take execution, trigger take() to revert with RatifierUnauthorized (line 355) since isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier] is now false, violating the invariant that offer validity must be stable once signed, causing scoped impact: fund freeze where valid signed offers become permanently unfillable after authorization revocation? Proof idea: sign offer; revoke authorization; call take; assert RatifierUnauthorized.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is a contract that calls IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(offer.maker, _signer) (as EcrecoverRatifier does at line 44), and the maker has authorized a new address that also has a valid signature for the same offer tree root, trigger take() to succeed with a different signer than originally intended, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the specific authorized signer, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill by a newly authorized address? Proof idea: sign offer with signer A; authorize signer B; provide signer B's signature; assert take succeeds.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is EcrecoverRatifier and ratifierData contains a valid signature for a different offer in the same Merkle tree (different leafIndex), trigger take() to succeed for the wrong offer by providing the correct leafIndex for the target offer but the signature for a different root, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the exact offer hash, causing scoped impact: offer replay where a signature for one offer authorizes a different offer? Proof idea: build Merkle tree with two offers; sign root for offer A; provide offer B with correct proof; assert InvalidProof revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take / IRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under preconditions where the ratifier is EcrecoverRatifier and the same signature is used for two different Midnight deployments (different MIDNIGHT addresses), trigger take() to succeed on the second deployment since EcrecoverRatifier uses block.chainid (not INITIAL_CHAIN_ID) in its domain separator (line 40), while Midnight uses INITIAL_CHAIN_ID for market IDs, violating the invariant that signatures must not be replayable across deployments, causing scoped impact: cross-deployment signature replay? Proof idea: deploy two Midnight instances; sign offer for ratifier1; attempt to use same signature with ratifier2 pointing to different MIDNIGHT.
